serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Yaki Chicken Udon with Dashi Broth weighs approximately 550 g and contains grilled chicken, thick udon noodles, and savoury dashi broth, representing a complete single-serve Japanese noodle meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477); Noodles, japanese, udon, cooked (FDC ID 169737); Soup, stock, chicken, home-prepared (FDC ID 171534); Mushrooms, shiitake, cooked (FDC ID 169242); composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries weighted by approximate dish composition: chicken thigh ~25%, udon noodles ~35%, dashi broth ~30%, vegetables and condiments ~10%. Input ai_generated GrabFood values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this USDA component estimate and have been cross-validated and retained. Sodium at 400 mg/100g is plausible for a soy-dashi broth dish and is consistent with USDA broth and soy sauce sodium data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Udon noodles, white wheat, boiled (GI ~55, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth as a composite dish. GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled udon noodles (white wheat, GI ~55, glucose scale), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls in the LOW category (GI ≤ 55). The presence of protein (chicken, ~8.2g/100g) and broth may further reduce the effective glycaemic response relative to plain noodles; GI 55 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 74.8) / 100 ≈ 41.1, where carbohydrate per 550g serving = 13.6 × 5.5 = 74.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g; estimated 2200 mg per 550g serving, approaching or exceeding the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and diluted dashi broth concentration, omit or reduce narutomaki (fish cake), and avoid consuming the full broth volume to limit sodium intake to a safer level per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat udon noodles with GI ~55 and 13.6g carbohydrate/100g (74.8g per serving); glycemic load per serving ~41, which is high
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion, substitute with konjac or wholegrain noodles where available, increase vegetable content (spinach, mushrooms), and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high glycemic load of a standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g (high) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 1.1g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.02g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sodium by requesting reduced broth and soy sauce, and may otherwise benefit from the lean protein content of grilled chicken and the low saturated fat profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce and dashi broth may worsen RA-related tissue oedema; however, chicken is a lean white meat and shiitake mushrooms provide anti-inflammatory beta-glucans
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce and broth concentration, retain shiitake mushrooms and any leafy greens for their anti-inflammatory properties, and omit processed narutomaki fish cake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yaki Chicken Udon with Dashi Broth is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of grilled or pan-seared chicken thigh served over thick, chewy wheat udon noodles in a savoury dashi-based broth. Dashi is a traditional Japanese stock made from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes), providing a delicate umami depth. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, contributing a lightly sweet-savoury flavour profile. Common accompaniments include shiitake mushrooms, narutomaki (Japanese fish cake), and spring onions. The dish is a classic Japanese comfort food, widely served in ramen and udon restaurants across Japan and in Japanese-cuisine establishments in Singapore. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 118 kcal, 8.2g protein, 3.6g fat, and 13.6g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400mg — elevated due to the soy-dashi broth. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low by University of Sydney classification) based on the udon noodle component, but the glycemic load per standard 550g bowl is approximately 41, which is high. Clinically, the dish is a concern for hypertension due to high sodium, and for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high glycemic load of refined wheat noodles. Saturated fat is low at 1.1g/100g, making the fat profile relatively favourable for cardiovascular health when sodium is managed. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to wheat udon noodles and soy sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for grilled chicken thigh, cooked wheat noodles, chicken broth, and shiitake mushrooms, weighted by approximate dish composition. The input GrabFood values (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) are broadly consistent with the USDA component estimate and have been cross-validated and retained. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled udon noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries: grilled chicken thigh (FDC ID 171477), cooked udon/wheat noodles (FDC ID 169737), chicken broth (FDC ID 171534), shiitake mushrooms (FDC ID 169242), weighted by approximate dish composition (chicken ~25%, noodles ~35%, broth ~30%, vegetables/condiments ~10%). Input ai_generated GrabFood values cross-validated and retained as consistent with USDA component estimates. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7) are USDA component-based estimates; not directly reported in input. 3. potassium_mg (220) is a USDA component-based estimate. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level data exists but composite dish-level values are not published. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled udon noodles (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 is LOW (threshold: low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70); gi_category corrected to 'low' from previous draft's erroneous 'medium'. No direct GI measurement for yaki chicken udon with dashi broth exists in an approved source. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed as dashi may contain non-halal fish products and mirin contains alcohol; is_halal set to false as a conservative default. 7. The dish is sold by 606D restaurant on GrabFood Singapore; country_of_origin is Singapore for this specific commercial preparation, though the cuisine is Japanese. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 (udon noodle dish) used as closest_parent anchor; FoodEx2 A0CKP (noodle soup dish with meat) used as closest_parent anchor. No codes invented. 9. This is a Japanese cuisine dish; mandatory multilingual requirement is Japanese native-script name only (G6). local_names.japanese is populated. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi are not required for Japanese cuisine and are correctly left null. aliases_by_language for chinese, malay, tamil, hindi are empty arrays as these languages are not mandatory for Japanese cuisine.
